note: Canonical parity prompts. `tokens` was filled by tokenizing `text` with the fork's llama-tokenize --ids (add-bos on, model default) against laguna-s-2.1-Q4_K_M.gguf. At parity time scripts/ref-dump.sh re-derives the authoritative ids from the eval-callback token echo (guaranteeing both the fork oracle and our logits-dump run on the identical sequence); the ids here are for reference and for driving logits-dump directly. BOS token id is 2.

prompts.note: Short code prompt (58 tokens). Fits in one prefill chunk and inside the 512-token SWA window (all sliding-window layers see the whole prompt).

prompts.text: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. In 2024, the team reported a 3.7x speedup on the Q4_K_M kernels, down from 128 ms to just 34 ms per token. Costs fell from $0.012 to $0.004 -- roughly a third. Not bad for a Tuesday afternoon of profiling!
prompts.note: 82 tokens of mixed prose, digits, currency, and punctuation. Still inside the SWA window; exercises the tokenizer's number/symbol handling.

prompts.text:
Sliding-window attention changes what a language model can see. In a full-attention layer, every position attends to every earlier position, so the cost of a forward pass grows with the square of the sequence length. That is fine for short prompts, but it becomes expensive once a prompt runs to thousands of tokens. A sliding-window layer instead restricts each position to a fixed span of recent context -- here, five hundred and twelve tokens -- so its cost grows only linearly with the sequence. The trade-off is that information from far earlier in the prompt cannot reach a sliding-window layer directly; it has to be carried forward through the residual stream by the full-attention layers that are interleaved among them.

Laguna arranges these two layer types in a repeating pattern. Every fourth layer, starting from the first, uses full attention; the three layers between each pair of full-attention layers use sliding-window attention. This means that any given token's influence can still propagate across the entire sequence, but it does so in hops: a full-attention layer gathers distant context, writes a summary into the residual stream, and the following sliding-window layers refine that summary locally before the next full-attention layer gathers again. The design keeps most of the compute cheap while preserving the long-range mixing that a purely local model would lack.

The two layer types also rotate their positional encodings differently. Full-attention layers apply a scaled rotary encoding tuned for long contexts, stretching the effective range of the position frequencies so that tokens far apart remain distinguishable. Sliding-window layers use a plain rotary encoding with no stretching, because within a five-hundred-and-twelve-token window there is no need to reach further. Getting this split right matters for correctness: if a verification harness feeds the model a prompt shorter than the window, the sliding-window and full-attention layers behave almost identically, and a bug in the windowing logic can hide. Only once the prompt exceeds the window does the masking diverge, and the two paths must be checked separately.

That is exactly why one of the canonical parity prompts is deliberately longer than the window. A short code snippet and a paragraph of ordinary prose both fit comfortably inside five hundred and twelve tokens, so they validate the arithmetic of a single dense block, the mixture-of-experts routing, the shared expert, and the attention gate. But they never exercise the case where a sliding-window layer must drop the oldest tokens from its view while a full-attention layer keeps them. A prompt that runs well past the window forces the key-value cache to evict entries for the windowed layers but not for the full-attention ones, and it forces the position-dependent masking to differ between the two. If the reference implementation and our own engine agree on the final logits for a prompt like this, we can be reasonably confident that the interleaved masking, the dual rotary encodings, and the cache bookkeeping are all correct together, not just in isolation. To make the check meaningful the text simply has to keep going for long enough, which is the entire purpose of this last, rather over-explained, paragraph.
prompts.note: 609 tokens -- deliberately exceeds the 512-token SWA window so the sliding-window eviction and interleaved masking diverge from full attention. This is the prompt that actually stresses the windowing path.
